Document [23] (Title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Surgical Considerations) When making a treatment plan for a questionable tooth, many factors come into play to decide whether the tooth should be treated and maintained, or extracted and replaced by an implant. From a periodontal standpoint, the prognosis of a tooth is determined by its amount of attachment loss and probing pocket depth. The progressive loss of periodontal attachment and increase in probe pocket depth are predictive of additional disease activity increases the likelihood of needing to be extracted. If the bone loss is too great with the periodontal loss, an implant would only be a possible replacement with successful bone volume augmentation. From a tooth structural standpoint, the prognosis of a tooth is determined by its amount of coronal structure remaining. A prosthetic crown can be placed on the tooth if there is sufficient structure remaining to retain the crown. Crown lengthening and extrusion can increase the amount of tooth structure for sufficient retention of a dental crown when appropriate. [17]

Document [27] (Title: Esophageal Diverticula -- Treatment Planning) Endoscopic myotomy (Dohlman Procedure) demands neck extension; therefore, it would not be an appropriate choice for patients with cervical stenosis who would not tolerate neck extension. The definitive management of symptomatic Zenker diverticulum is the surgical approach. The decision to use either an open or endoscopic transoral approach (rigid endoscope or a flexible endoscope) is made by clinicians based on several factors. The most important factors to be considered are 1. the ability to visualize the ZD and septum endoscopically, 2. patient's body habitus, 3. the ZD pouch feature in relation to the posterior wall, and 4. local expertise. Accordingly, patients with short necks decreased hyomental distance, and/or those patients with obesity are most often managed with an open approach. However, diverticula with less than 2 cm length are usually managed with endoscopic methods.

Document [14] (Title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2025) Cervical cerclage suture present, polyp of cervix affecting pregnancy, previous operation to cervix affecting pregnancy, and rigid cervix uteri affecting pregnancy are all findings that have the cervix uteri as their anatomic site. These entities represent structural or pathological conditions of the cervix that can influence pregnancy outcomes. A cervical cerclage suture is placed to reinforce a weakened cervix and prevent premature dilation, while a cervical polyp can interfere with implantation or cause bleeding during gestation. Prior cervical surgery may alter cervical integrity and increase the risk of cervical insufficiency, and a rigid cervix can impede normal cervical remodeling required for labor. Recognizing that each of these findings is located in the cervix uteri helps clinicians assess cervical health, anticipate potential obstetric complications, and tailor management strategies to support a safe pregnancy.
